expectations.text: Classifies input as technical design (not PRD or scope doc) and proceeds
expectations.evidence: Overview.md states '16 design decisions from the architecture doc are carried through into the issues' and references 'the design doc's phased build plan.' The output treats the input as a technical architecture document and proceeds to decompose it into implementation issues without attempting PRD or scope activities.

expectations.text: Issues reference actual file paths, line numbers, and type signatures from the rangeops codebase
expectations.evidence: File paths are present throughout (e.g., 'apps/web/src/lib/db/schema/waivers.ts', 'apps/web/src/lib/customers.ts'). However, NO issue references specific line numbers — not a single 'line N' or 'L123' appears in any of the 16 issues. Type signatures are also absent — type names are mentioned (e.g., WaiverTemplateNotFound, signWaiverSchema) but no actual TypeScript signatures like function signatures or interface definitions from the codebase. The expectation requires all three: file paths, line numbers, and type signatures.

expectations.text: Issues are vertical slices (thin end-to-end), not horizontal layers (all schema, then all API, then all UI)
expectations.evidence: The decomposition is explicitly horizontal. Phase 1 is labeled 'Schema + Template Management' — Issue 1 is purely DB schema, Issue 2 is purely error types and validation schemas, Issue 3 is purely feature flag, Issue 4 is purely service layer, Issue 5 is purely settings UI. Phase 2 similarly layers: Issue 6 is service layer, Issue 7 is UI page, Issue 8 is middleware, Issue 9 is email. A vertical slice would combine schema + service + UI for a single feature flow (e.g., 'Template creation end-to-end: add schema columns, service function, and settings UI form'). Instead, every issue is a single architectural layer.

Counted files per issue: Issue 1: 4 files (schema/waivers.ts, schema/index.ts, id.ts, customers.ts). Issue 2: 2 files (errors.ts, validation.ts). Issue 3: 1-2 files. Issue 4: 1 primary file (lib/waivers.ts). Issue 5: ~5 files (page.tsx, actions.ts, layout, queries.ts, components). Issue 6: 1-2 files. Issue 7: ~4 files. Issue 8: 1-2 files. Issue 9: 2-3 files. Issue 10: 3 files. Issue 11: 3-4 files. Issue 12: 2-3 files. Issues 13-16: 1 file each (test files). All within 1-6 range, none exceeding 8.

expectations.text: Issues have explicit dependency ordering — schema before API, API before UI, cross-cutting after what it wires into
expectations.evidence: Each phase table in overview.md lists explicit dependencies per issue. Issue 1 (schema): no deps. Issue 4 (service): depends on 1+2. Issue 5 (UI): depends on 1+2+3+4. Issue 6 (signing service): depends on 1+2. Issue 7 (signing page): depends on 1+2+3+6. Issue 8 (rate limiting): depends on 7. Issue 9 (email): depends on 6. Tests (13-16) depend on their respective service/UI issues. The dependency graph on lines 52-73 visualizes the critical path. Ordering correctly enforces schema -> API -> UI -> cross-cutting.

expectations.text: Each issue has: What to build, Files to modify/create (with paths), Context (patterns to follow, key types), Acceptance criteria (checkboxes), Verification commands
expectations.evidence: Four of five components are present: (1) 'What to build' — every issue has a Summary section. (2) 'Files to modify/create' — present with paths (e.g., Issue 1 lists 'apps/web/src/lib/db/schema/waivers.ts'). (3) 'Context' — present via Context and Technical Notes sections (e.g., Issue 4: 'Follow the pattern in customers.ts'). (4) 'Acceptance criteria with checkboxes' — all 16 issues use '- [ ]' format. However, (5) 'Verification commands' is MISSING. No issue has a dedicated verification section. Issues 13-16 mention 'All tests pass with vitest run' as an acceptance criterion line item, but issues 1-12 have zero verification commands (no 'pnpm drizzle-kit generate', 'pnpm test', 'pnpm typecheck', etc.).

expectations.text: Overview includes dependency graph (mermaid), execution order table, and plan coverage map
expectations.evidence: Dependency graph is present but NOT in mermaid format — it's ASCII art (lines 52-73 of overview.md use plain text with arrows like '|', 'v', and indentation). The expectation specifically requires mermaid. Execution order tables are present (phase tables on lines 11-48 with issue numbers, names, and dependencies). Plan coverage map is partially present — 'Design Decisions Preserved' section (lines 99-108) maps some design decisions to issues, and metrics.json lists covered requirements, but there is no systematic coverage map in overview.md showing which issue covers which requirement/feature.

expectations.text: Each issue classified as AFK (agent autonomous) or HITL (needs human review)
expectations.evidence: No issue contains any AFK or HITL classification. Searched all 16 issue files — none mention 'AFK', 'HITL', 'autonomous', 'human review', or any equivalent classification. This metadata is completely absent from every issue.

claims.evidence: Counted 16 issue files in the issues/ directory and three phases in overview.md (Phase 1: Issues 1-5,13; Phase 2: Issues 6-9,14; Phase 3: Issues 10-12,15-16).

claims.claim: All 16 design decisions from the architecture doc are carried through
claims.evidence: Overview.md references 8 specific design decisions (1, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 12, 14) and metrics.json lists all 16 as covered. However, without access to the original design doc, cannot verify whether the claimed coverage is accurate or if the mapping is correct. Only 8 of 16 are explicitly traced in overview.md.

claims.claim: Issues can be handed off to a coding agent independently
claims.evidence: Each issue has sufficient context: file paths, acceptance criteria, technical notes with patterns to follow, and explicit dependency lists. An agent with codebase access could implement any issue after its dependencies are complete.
metrics.json claims FR-1 through FR-31 are covered. Without the original PRD, this claim cannot be independently verified.

claims.claim: Issues 1, 2, 3 can all be done in parallel
claims.evidence: Issue 1 (schema), Issue 2 (error types/validation), and Issue 3 (feature flag) each list 'None' as dependencies and touch non-overlapping files.

suggestions.assertion: Issues are vertical slices (thin end-to-end), not horizontal layers
suggestions.reason: This is a strong discriminating assertion. However, there's an argument that for foundational infrastructure (DB schema, error types), horizontal layering IS the right approach and vertical slices only make sense for feature-level work. The eval might benefit from nuance: 'Feature issues are vertical slices' vs 'infrastructure issues may be horizontal.' As written, it correctly catches the output's purely horizontal decomposition.

suggestions.assertion: Issues reference actual file paths, line numbers, and type signatures from the rangeops codebase
suggestions.reason: This assertion conflates three separate qualities. File paths are abundantly present and high-quality. Line numbers and type signatures are absent but arguably less critical for greenfield feature work (most files are NEW, so there are no existing line numbers to reference). Consider splitting into: (1) file paths present, (2) references existing code patterns with specifics (line numbers or type signatures where modifying existing files).

No assertion checks whether issues include estimated complexity or size. The output has 16 issues ranging from trivial (Issue 3: feature flag, ~20 lines) to large (Issue 7: public signing page with form, validation, sanitization, confirmation). Size estimation would help with sprint planning and agent task assignment.

No assertion checks whether the issues correctly handle the boundary between new files and modifications to existing files. The output does distinguish these (overview.md Key Files Touched section), but this is an important quality signal — getting this wrong means agents will overwrite existing code or miss integration points.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-targeted at structural qualities (slicing strategy, file scope, dependency ordering, completeness). The vertical-slice and AFK/HITL assertions are highly discriminating — they test for qualities that require deliberate design, not just output volume. The line-numbers assertion may be too strict for greenfield features where most files are new.
